Ready to start planning? Bookmark this guide, share it with whoever is helping you, and come back to the Prettiee Decor blog for decoration inspiration across every room and every celebration. We are here to make beautiful feel achievable at every budget.

title: Wedding Decoration Trends 2026: What's In and What's Out for Your Big Day
author: Harpreet Kaur
excerpt: Discover the hottest 2026 wedding decoration trends—from moody botanicals to bold color palettes—and find out what to ditch for your perfect big day.
content:
Wedding Decoration Trends 2026: What's In and What's Out for Your Big Day

After spending over a decade styling weddings across the globe—from rooftop terraces under open skies to grand ballrooms draped in thousands of blooms—I can tell you one thing with absolute certainty: the wedding decoration world never stands still. Every year, couples push the boundaries of what a wedding can look and feel like, and 2026 is no different. In fact, this year might be the most exciting shift in aesthetic I have seen in a long time.

I have sat across from hundreds of couples in planning sessions, watched faces light up at a perfectly placed floral arch, and—yes—gently talked a few people out of trends that were already fading by the time they fell in love with them on social media. My job is not just to make things beautiful. It is to make sure your wedding still feels timeless in your photo album ten years from now.

So whether you are deep in the planning trenches or just beginning to dream, this guide is your honest, decorator-approved breakdown of what is dominating 2026 wedding decor, what is finally on its way out, how much things actually cost, and what tools will help you pull it all off.

What's In vs. What's Out: 2026 Wedding Decoration Trends at a Glance

Before we dive deep, here is a quick-reference comparison of where the wedding world is headed this year. I have pulled this directly from the dozens of consultations, venue walkthroughs, and real weddings I have been part of this season.

Trend Area ✅ What's In (2026) ❌ What's Out
Color Palette Deep jewel tones: emerald, sapphire, burgundy, ochre All-white and blush minimalism
Florals Moody botanicals, dried pampas, sculptural arrangements Uniform white rose towers and tight posy arrangements
Lighting Warm Edison bulbs, dramatic candlelight, neon signs Flat overhead fairy lights everywhere
Sustainability Potted plants, seed favors, rented decor, local blooms Single-use plastic table confetti and foam props
Table Settings Mixed textures: rattan, linen, aged brass, velvet runners Matching everything — uniform chair sashes and table covers
Ceremony Backdrop Organic arches with asymmetric greenery, drapery clouds Perfectly symmetrical balloon arches
Signage Hand-painted wooden boards, terracotta lettering Acrylic mirror signs with gold vinyl text
Seating Mix-and-match vintage chairs, lounge vignettes at reception Rows of identical gold chiavari chairs
Favors Edible, plantable, or charitable donation cards Personalized keychains and miniature frames
Overall Aesthetic Intentional eclecticism — curated but relaxed Over-coordinated "Pinterest-perfect" uniformity

Deep Dive: Top 5 Wedding Decoration Trends for 2026

1. Jewel-Toned Color Palettes

Gone are the days when every wedding felt like a cloud of white and blush. In 2026, couples are leaning boldly into deep, saturated colors—and I am absolutely here for it. I had a client this spring who chose emerald green and antique gold as her palette. When guests walked in and saw the deep velvet runners, the forest-green pillar candles, and the rich floral arrangements bursting with dahlias and eucalyptus, three people started crying before the ceremony even began. That is the power of color used intentionally.

  • How to achieve it: Start with your linens and florals, then build everything else around those tones. Do not feel like every element needs to match — let the palette breathe with off-white and neutral textures.
  • Worth spending on: Quality velvet or linen table runners and real statement florals in your accent color.
  • Skip the splurge: You do not need jewel-toned chairs — a pop in the centerpieces and linens is enough.
  • Cost range: Low: $800–$1,200 | Medium: $2,500–$4,000 | High: $7,000–$15,000+

2. Moody Botanical and Sculptural Florals

The era of matching, round, tight floral arrangements sitting in the center of tables like decorative paperweights is officially over. In 2026, the most talked-about florals are wild, sculptural, and almost architectural. Think long cascading arrangements that spill over the table edge, dried pampas grass mixed with fresh blooms, and hanging installations that create a canopy effect overhead.

  • How to achieve it: Work with your florist to design at least two or three "hero" arrangements that anchor the room, and let the rest be simpler. One jaw-dropping floral chandelier does more work than ten matching centerpieces.
  • Worth spending on: A statement floral installation behind the head table or ceremony arch. This is the piece that will be in every photo.
  • Skip the splurge: Supplement with high-quality dried florals (pampas, bunny tails, dried citrus slices) — they are a fraction of the cost and photograph beautifully.
  • Cost range: Low: $600–$1,000 | Medium: $2,000–$4,500 | High: $8,000–$20,000+

3. Warm, Layered Lighting

I always tell couples: lighting is the one thing guests will feel without knowing why. A wedding lit entirely with flat overhead fixtures feels like a school cafeteria. But the same room transformed with warm Edison bulbs strung at varying heights, pillar candles on every surface, and a single glowing neon sign above the dance floor? That is a wedding people will talk about for years.

  • How to achieve it: Think in layers — ambient (overhead), accent (candles, uplighting), and decorative (a neon sign or lanterns). Each layer adds depth and intimacy.
  • Worth spending on: A professional lighting rental company for your reception space. This is genuinely one of the highest-ROI decisions in your decor budget.
  • Skip the splurge: Fairy lights draped uniformly across a ceiling. It is overdone and the effect is flat compared to layered alternatives.
  • Cost range: Low: $400–$800 | Medium: $1,500–$3,000 | High: $5,000–$12,000

4. Sustainable and Eco-Conscious Decor

This is not just a trend — it is a genuine values shift I have witnessed accelerate dramatically in recent years. More and more couples come to me asking how to make their wedding beautiful without leaving behind hundreds of kilograms of single-use waste. The good news? Sustainable decor is not a compromise. It is often more beautiful and more personal than its throwaway counterparts.

  • How to achieve it: Rent where you can (chairs, linens, tableware, large decor pieces). Choose potted plants as centerpieces that guests can take home. Use seasonal, locally sourced blooms. Swap plastic table confetti for dried petals.
  • Worth spending on: A rental company with a wide, curated inventory. The upfront cost is higher than buying cheap, but the aesthetic quality and reduced waste are both worth it.
  • Skip the splurge: Branded "eco" favors that cost more because of the marketing. Seed packets from a local nursery cost almost nothing and are genuinely meaningful.
  • Cost range: Low: $500–$900 | Medium: $1,800–$3,500 | High: $6,000–$13,000

5. Intentional Eclecticism in Seating and Table Styling

The most memorable receptions I have designed recently have one thing in common: nothing perfectly matches, and that is entirely on purpose. A mix of vintage bentwood chairs alongside modern acrylic ghost chairs. Rattan charger plates under delicate porcelain. Aged brass candlesticks next to a linen napkin folded with a sprig of rosemary. This curated mismatching communicates personality and warmth in a way that perfectly coordinated settings simply cannot.

  • How to achieve it: Pick two or three "anchor" materials (wood, linen, brass, velvet, rattan) and mix them consistently across the room. The key is cohesion through palette, not uniformity through matching pieces.
  • Worth spending on: One or two distinctive chair styles for the sweetheart table or head table. These are the focal point of most reception photos.
  • Skip the splurge: Sourcing dozens of different vintage chairs from multiple vendors is logistically exhausting. Pick two styles and alternate them — same effect, fraction of the effort.
  • Cost range: Low: $700–$1,200 | Medium: $2,500–$5,000 | High: $9,000–$18,000

Budget Breakdown by Trend

One of the most common questions I get is: how much should I actually budget for each element? Here is a realistic breakdown based on what I see couples spending across different budget tiers in 2026. These are estimates for a wedding of approximately 80–120 guests.

Decor Element 💰 Low Budget (USD) 💰💰 Mid Budget (USD) 💰💰💰 High Budget (USD)
Ceremony Arch / Backdrop $300 – $600 $1,200 – $2,500 $5,000 – $12,000
Floral Centerpieces (per table) $40 – $80 $120 – $250 $350 – $800
Lighting (full reception) $400 – $800 $1,500 – $3,000 $5,000 – $12,000
Table Linen & Runners $200 – $500 $800 – $1,800 $3,000 – $7,000
Seating (rental) $400 – $900 $1,500 – $3,500 $5,000 – $14,000
Signage & Stationery Display $100 – $250 $400 – $900 $1,500 – $4,000
Sweetheart / Head Table Styling $200 – $500 $700 – $1,800 $3,000 – $8,000
Estimated Total Decor $1,640 – $3,630 $6,220 – $13,750 $22,850 – $57,000

Note: These figures are estimates and will vary based on your location, vendor availability, seasonality, and the scale of your wedding. Always get at least three quotes from local vendors before finalizing your budget.

Best Tools and Materials for Achieving 2026 Wedding Trends

Over the years I have built up a reliable toolkit — both physical materials and vendor relationships — that I return to again and again when building a wedding look. Here is what I recommend for the 2026 aesthetic:

Floral and Botanical Materials

  • Pampas grass (dried): Wildly versatile, cost-effective, and photographically stunning. Buy in bulk for the best pricing — around $3–$8 per stem depending on size.
  • Dried citrus slices and seed pods: Perfect accent pieces for table arrangements. Inexpensive and add incredible textural depth.
  • Seasonal fresh blooms: Always ask your florist what is in season. In-season flowers cost 30–60% less than out-of-season blooms and are fresher and more vibrant.
  • Floral wire, floral foam alternatives (chicken wire or moss): For the eco-conscious, foam-free armatures using chicken wire, moss, or wooden frames are a must in 2026.

Lighting Equipment and Accessories

  • Edison bulb string lights (warm white, 2200K–2700K): The gold standard for intimate, warm reception lighting. Rent from a professional AV or event company for the best result.
  • Pillar candles (unscented, various heights): Group three to five at varying heights for maximum drama. Beeswax candles have a warm golden flame that flatters everyone.
  • LED uplighting in amber and warm rose tones: Position around the perimeter of the venue walls for a professional-level glow-up effect. Many lighting rental companies include these in packages.
  • Custom neon signs: Budget around $200–$500 for a custom piece. It serves as a statement prop and a guest photo backdrop all evening.

Table and Linen Materials

  • Linen table runners (natural or washed linen): My absolute favourite. They drape beautifully, photograph in a warm, editorial way, and set a tone of relaxed elegance.
  • Rattan charger plates: Gorgeous layered under ceramic or porcelain dinner plates. A set of 100 rattan chargers can be rented for approximately $120–$250.
  • Aged brass and matte black candlesticks: Mix them. Seriously. The combination of warm metal and matte black is one of the most striking tablescaping choices you can make right now.
  • Dried flower and herb napkin ties: Skip the napkin rings. A small sprig of dried lavender, rosemary, or cotton tied with twine costs almost nothing and adds enormous charm.

Ceremony and Backdrop Structures

  • Wooden or bamboo arch frames: A sturdy hexagonal or rounded wooden arch is endlessly adaptable. Dress it differently and it works for every aesthetic from boho to formal.
  • Drapery fabric (chiffon, voile, silk organza): Flowing fabric panels behind the ceremony space are having a major moment. Soft whites, ivory, and warm terracotta tones are all working beautifully.
  • Macramé panels: Making a comeback in a more refined form than their 2018–2020 versions. Look for large-scale, neutral-toned pieces with subtle geometric patterns.

Real Wedding Examples

Wedding 1: Emerald and Brass Garden Celebration — $9,500 Decor Budget

Earlier this year I worked with a couple who had a deep love for nature and a tight but thoughtful budget. We centered everything around an emerald, ochre, and aged brass palette. The ceremony arch was a simple wooden oval frame dressed asymmetrically with trailing smilax vine, burgundy dahlias, and dried bunny tails. For the reception, we rented mismatched bentwood and cane chairs, laid natural linen runners on round tables, and built centerpieces from a mix of fresh dahlias and dried grasses in terracotta pots — which guests took home afterward. Lighting was warm Edison string lights at varying heights with clusters of pillar candles. The whole look was lush, intentional, and felt absolutely nothing like a standard rental package. Total decor spend: $9,500 for 95 guests.

Wedding 2: Coastal Moody Botanical — $4,200 Decor Budget

This couple had a tight budget but exceptional taste and a lot of DIY willingness. We focused the spend on three hero elements: a dramatic pampas grass and dried floral ceremony backdrop ($600), professional warm lighting for the reception hall ($1,100), and linen runners with rented rattan chargers for all tables ($850). Everything else — signage, napkin styling, candle clusters, and floral accents — was DIY using bulk dried stems, beeswax candles from a local market, and hand-lettered wooden signs the groom painted himself. The result was genuinely stunning. Guests could not believe the total came in under $4,500, and the photos are some of the most striking I have seen from a modest budget wedding.

Wedding 3: Jewel-Toned Luxury Ballroom — $34,000 Decor Budget

On the other end of the spectrum, I had the pleasure of designing a full ballroom wedding for 140 guests centered on a sapphire blue, deep plum, and antique gold palette. The ceremony space featured a 12-foot cloud drapery installation behind the couple — cascading ivory and cream chiffon panels with a chandelier of hanging orchids and smilax. Each reception table had tall glass vase centerpieces filled with deep blue hydrangea, plum lisianthus, and gold-tipped eucalyptus. Custom uplighting bathed the entire venue in a warm amber glow, and a custom neon sign reading the couple's wedding date anchored the photo booth corner. It was theatrical, romantic, and genuinely unlike anything guests had seen before. Every dollar was felt.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q1: How far in advance should I book a wedding decorator for a 2026 wedding?

Ideally, 9–12 months in advance for peak wedding season dates (spring and autumn especially). Popular decorators in most markets are fully booked 8 months out for Saturdays. If you are flexible on date and day of the week, you may find quality decorators available with 4–6 months' notice. The earlier you book, the better your vendor selection and pricing.

Q2: What is the single most impactful decor investment for a limited budget?

Lighting, every time. A great florist will tell you the same thing. You can have modest florals and simple table settings, but if the room is lit beautifully with warm, layered light, the whole space feels elevated. Conversely, even the most expensive flowers fall flat under harsh, flat venue lighting. If you can only splurge on one thing, make it lighting.

Q3: Are dried florals a budget option or a genuine trend choice in 2026?

Both, honestly. Dried florals — pampas grass, bunny tails, dried citrus, wheat stalks, lotus heads — are genuinely on-trend in their own right in 2026. They are not just a budget substitute; many decorators (including myself) prefer them in certain compositions because of their sculptural quality and muted, editorial colour palette. They are also eco-friendly, as there is no waste after the event. So yes, they save money, but do not treat them as second-best — they are first-choice for the right aesthetic.

Q4: Should I follow trends or choose a timeless look?

My honest advice: choose a look you genuinely love, but avoid the most fleeting micro-trends. Bold colour palettes, warm lighting, and organic florals are not gimmicks — they are rooted in design principles that have always worked. What ages poorly are hyper-specific props (certain viral balloon configurations, specific novelty items) that are deeply tied to a single cultural moment. If you love a trend because it genuinely resonates with your personal taste, go for it. If you are choosing it purely because it is popular right now, pause and ask whether you would have loved it five years ago.

Q5: Can I mix 2026 trends with a classic or traditional wedding aesthetic?

Absolutely — and this is actually one of my favourite things to do. A classic white gown and traditional ceremony can coexist beautifully with a moody botanical reception aesthetic. Mix a timeless structure (formal venue, elegant stationery, a classic cake) with contemporary decorative choices (jewel-toned linens, sculptural florals, layered candlelight). The contrast is what makes it interesting and uniquely yours.

Q6: How do I avoid the wedding looking outdated in photos five years from now?

Focus on quality materials and organic elements over trend-specific novelties. Natural textures (wood, linen, stone, dried botanicals), warm lighting, and a considered colour palette photograph in a way that feels timeless regardless of the year. The things that date wedding photos most quickly are hyper-trend-specific props, specific font and signage fads, and very uniform, artificial-looking decor. When in doubt, choose natural over synthetic, textured over flat, and warm over cool.

Final Thoughts: Your Wedding, Your Story

If there is one thing I want you to take away from this guide, it is this: the best wedding decoration is the kind that feels unmistakably like you. Trends are a starting point, not a script. Use them to spark ideas, challenge your assumptions, and give language to things you already love — but always filter them through your own story.

Whether you are working with a $2,000 decor budget or $20,000, the principles are the same: invest in lighting, anchor the room with one or two statement pieces, choose materials that have texture and warmth, and do not try to fill every inch of space. Elegance is often about what you leave out.

If you are still exploring ideas and looking for more inspiration tailored to specific spaces and styles, I have a few resources you might find genuinely helpful:

  • For couples planning an event in East Africa, do not miss our deep-dive on Wedding Decoration Ideas for Dar es Salaam in 2026 — it covers local vendors, seasonal florals, and budget guidance specific to the region.
  • If you are redecorating your home around your wedding and want to refresh your bedroom on a budget, our Bedroom Decoration Tips and Budget Guide is a practical companion to your planning process.

I hope this guide saves you time, money, and a few planning headaches. Feel free to reach out through Prettiee Decor if you would like a personalised consultation — I love nothing more than helping a couple build a look that is entirely their own.

— Harpreet Kaur, Lead Decorator at Prettiee Decor

title: Balloon Decoration Ideas for Every Celebration: Weddings, Birthdays & More (2026 Guide)
author: Harpreet Kaur
excerpt: From organic balloon arches to foil number clusters, discover top balloon decoration ideas for weddings, birthdays, baby showers, and corporate events in 2026.
content:
Balloon Decoration Ideas for Every Celebration: Weddings, Birthdays & More (2026 Guide)

I will be honest with you: when I first started decorating events, I underestimated balloons. I thought of them as the cheap filler you grab at the last minute from a petrol station — something you do when you have run out of ideas. Then I walked into a venue where a colleague had installed a 15-foot organic balloon cloud over the sweetheart table, layered in dusty rose, champagne, and cream, trailing smoked eucalyptus, and I immediately rethought everything I believed about this medium.

Balloons, done right, are one of the most versatile, high-impact, and genuinely joyful decoration tools available to any event decorator or DIY host. After building hundreds of balloon installations across every event type — weddings, milestone birthdays, corporate launches, baby showers, intimate bridal brunches — I have learned what works, what does not, and exactly how much everything should cost you in 2026.

This guide covers all of it. Whether you are planning to DIY a birthday balloon garland this weekend or you want to understand what you are paying a professional decorator for, you are in the right place.

Balloon Decoration Ideas by Event Type

Weddings

Balloons at weddings have shed their children's-party reputation entirely. In 2026, the most sought-after wedding balloon looks are organic, sculptural, and deliberately colour-matched to the couple's full decor palette. Here is what is working beautifully right now:

  • Organic balloon arch over the ceremony backdrop: A freeform arrangement of latex balloons in varying sizes, clustered to look like a natural cloud or crescent. No two are identical, which is exactly the point. Mix matte, metallic, and clear balloons for maximum depth. Estimated materials cost: $80–$180 depending on size and balloon quality.
  • Balloon ceiling installation for the reception: A canopy of helium balloons at different heights above the dance floor or reception tables. This is breathtaking in photos and creates an immersive experience for guests. This is one I always recommend leaving to a professional — the rigging and helium volume involved make it genuinely complex. Professional cost: $400–$1,200+.
  • Balloon columns flanking the aisle: Tall spiral columns at the ceremony entrance or along the aisle. Classic, structural, and surprisingly affordable. A pair of columns runs approximately $60–$140 in materials or $150–$350 professionally installed.

Birthday Parties

Birthdays are where balloons truly get to shine without restraint. The most popular looks I execute for birthdays are also among the most DIY-friendly — though some require more patience than people expect.

  • Balloon garland wall backdrop: The single most photographed birthday decoration of the last three years, and still going strong. A dense, layered garland of latex balloons in your theme colours mounted against a wall. Perfect for the cake table or gift table. Materials: $40–$90. Professional: $120–$280.
  • Giant foil number balloons: The 30, 40, 50 — you know the ones. They are still wildly popular because they are effortlessly readable in photos and require almost no skill to set up. Foil number sets typically cost $15–$35 depending on size and finish.
  • Balloon bouquet centrepieces: Three to five balloons (a mix of latex and foil) tied together with ribbon and weighted at the base. Simple, affordable, and easily colour-matched. Budget roughly $8–$20 per bouquet for materials.

Bridal Showers

Bridal showers call for something softer and more refined than a birthday party but still festive and celebratory. My go-to combination is an organic balloon cluster in blush, ivory, and champagne tones mounted behind the gift or dessert table, paired with two or three helium foil balloons spelling "BRIDE" or the bride's first name.

  • Organic cluster with foil accent balloons: A relaxed grouping of latex balloons with a couple of large foil statement pieces (hearts, stars, letter balloons). Materials: $45–$85.
  • Balloon ceiling fringe installation: Hanging ribbon streamers with small latex balloons tied at the end, creating a soft, dreamy overhead effect. Materials: $25–$50.

Corporate Events

Corporate events are where I ask clients to invest a little more in quality, because everything will be photographed and shared. The look needs to be polished and brand-consistent. For corporate work, I almost always recommend professional installation.

  • Brand-coloured balloon columns or towers at the entrance: Structural, impactful, and one of the first things guests see. Using your brand's exact Pantone colours (many premium balloon suppliers offer custom colour matching) creates an incredibly cohesive impression. Professional installation: $200–$600 per pair.
  • Step-and-repeat balloon wall backdrop: A dense organic balloon wall covering a 6–8 foot section for branded photography and media coverage. This is complex, time-consuming, and not a DIY project for most people. Professional: $500–$1,500+ depending on dimensions and complexity.

Baby Showers

Baby showers are among my favourite events to decorate with balloons because the colour palette possibilities are so joyful — and because foil balloons in the shapes of clouds, stars, moons, and animals are genuinely adorable and incredibly easy to work with.

  • Giant foil star and cloud balloons with latex cluster: Mix a 34-inch foil star or cloud balloon with a cluster of coordinating latex balloons in soft pastel tones. Wildly effective and budget-friendly. Materials: $30–$60.
  • "It's a Boy / It's a Girl" letter balloons: Gold, rose gold, or silver foil letter balloons spelling a short phrase. A timeless reveal moment. Cost: $20–$45 for a full set.

DIY Balloon Arch Tutorial (5 Steps)

Let me be upfront: a balloon arch is not as simple as it looks on video. The first one I ever built took me four hours and looked nothing like the inspiration photo. The second one took two hours and looked pretty good. By the tenth, I had it down to 45 minutes. The learning curve is real, but it is absolutely achievable for a home DIY project. Here is the simplified process:

What You Will Need

  • 100–150 latex balloons in your chosen colours (11-inch is the standard size)
  • Balloon decorating strip (a plastic strip with holes — this is the secret weapon)
  • Hand balloon pump (do not try to inflate 100 balloons by mouth)
  • Glue dots or low-temperature hot glue gun
  • Command hooks or a sturdy anchor point (wall, stand, or door frame)
  • Fishing line or clear string for hanging small accent balloons

Step 1 — Inflate Your Balloons

Inflate your balloons in mixed sizes — roughly 60% at full size (11 inches), 30% slightly smaller, and 10% quite small (5–6 inches). This size variation is what gives an organic arch its natural, cloud-like appearance. Tie each balloon once inflated. This step takes longer than you expect — budget 30–45 minutes for 100 balloons.

Step 2 — Thread the Decorating Strip

Push the knot of each balloon through one of the holes in the plastic decorating strip. Work in colour clusters of three or four, then alternate. Alternate left and right sides of the strip as you go to create density. This is where most beginners go wrong — do not space the balloons too far apart. Crowd them slightly for that full, lush look.

Step 3 — Build Your Base Shape

Anchor one end of your balloon strip to your starting point (command hook, arch stand, or door frame). Gently curve the strip into your desired arch shape and anchor the other end. The strip is flexible and forgiving — you can reshape it as you go.

Step 4 — Fill the Gaps

This is where the small balloons come in. Use glue dots to attach individual smaller balloons into any gaps between the larger ones. Step back frequently and look at the overall shape. Keep filling until it looks dense and full — sparse arches are the most common DIY mistake.

Step 5 — Add Finishing Touches

Tuck in any accent elements: a few foil balloons, some greenery stems, dried flowers, or ribbon tails. If you are adding helium balloons, tie them to fishing line and attach near the top of the arch. Stand back, adjust, and resist the urge to keep fiddling — sometimes done is better than perfect.

Total materials cost for a 6-foot DIY arch: approximately $50–$120 depending on balloon quality and any accent pieces.

Balloon Types Comparison

Balloon Type Best For Avg. Cost (per unit) Lifespan Notes
Latex Arches, garlands, clusters, columns $0.15 – $0.60 Air-filled: 1–2 weeks. Helium: 12–24 hrs Biodegradable; latex allergy risk; avoid direct sunlight outdoors
Foil (Mylar) Numbers, letters, shapes, statement pieces $2 – $12 Helium: 3–5 days. Air-filled: several weeks Not biodegradable; reusable; sharp edges on some shapes; great colour retention
Bubble (Bobo) Elegant centrepieces, floral inserts, modern looks $3 – $8 Air: weeks. Helium: 2–4 days Clear PVC material; can hold small objects or florals inside; premium appearance
Organic / Jumbo Latex Ceiling clouds, large statement installations $1.50 – $5 Air: 1–2 weeks. Helium: 12–18 hrs 36-inch sizes; dramatic visual impact; requires more helium; best air-filled for arches

Tools You Need (With Estimated Costs)

The difference between a frustrating balloon project and a smooth one almost always comes down to having the right tools. Here is exactly what I use and what each item costs:

  • Electric balloon pump: If you are inflating more than 30 balloons, a hand pump will exhaust you. An electric tabletop pump costs $25–$55 and pays for itself on the first project. I use mine at every single event.
  • Hand pump (manual): Good backup and great for small jobs. Cost: $5–$12.
  • Balloon decorating strip: A bag of 16-foot strips (usually 2–3 strips per pack) costs $6–$12. Essential for any garland or arch project.
  • Glue dots (low-tack): These are specifically designed for balloons — they hold firmly without popping the latex. A pack of 200 dots costs around $5–$8.
  • Low-temperature hot glue gun: For attaching larger accent pieces and greenery. Any craft store model works — cost: $8–$20. Never use a high-temperature gun near balloons.
  • Balloon sizing template: A simple cardboard or plastic ring with holes at various diameters (5", 9", 11") to ensure consistent balloon sizing. Cost: $4–$10, or make your own from cardboard for free.
  • Fishing line (clear, 20lb): For hanging individual balloons or small clusters from ceilings. A spool costs $3–$7 and lasts for dozens of events.
  • Command hooks (various sizes): For mounting arches and installations without damaging walls. A pack of assorted hooks costs $8–$15.
  • Helium tank (portable rental): For helium-filled balloons, a small portable tank fills approximately 30–50 standard latex balloons. Rental cost: $35–$65 per day from most party supply stores.

Starter toolkit total (pump, strip, glue dots, hooks, fishing line): approximately $45–$90.

Budget Breakdown by Event Size

Element 💰 Low Budget (DIY) 💰💰 Mid Budget (Semi-Pro) 💰💰💰 High Budget (Full Pro)
Balloon arch / garland (6 ft) $50 – $90 $150 – $280 $350 – $700
Balloon columns (pair) $30 – $60 $100 – $220 $200 – $500
Balloon wall / backdrop (6×8 ft) $120 – $200 $350 – $600 $700 – $1,500
Balloon bouquet centrepieces (each) $8 – $15 $20 – $45 $50 – $120
Ceiling balloon cloud / canopy Not recommended as DIY $300 – $600 $700 – $2,000+
Foil number / letter set $15 – $35 $35 – $70 $70 – $150
Full event balloon package (typical) $80 – $200 $400 – $900 $1,200 – $4,000+

Safety Tips and Considerations

I include this section in every balloon guide I write because it genuinely matters — and because I have seen things go wrong when people skip these steps.

  • Latex allergy awareness: Latex allergies are more common than most people realise and can range from mild skin irritation to severe anaphylactic reactions. If you know any guests have latex allergies, use foil and bubble balloons exclusively for your event. Always ask your client or host about allergies during planning — I do this without exception.
  • Outdoor heat and sun: Balloons expand in heat and pop when exposed to direct sunlight, especially dark-coloured latex. I have had a beautifully constructed outdoor arch reduced to shredded rubber within 90 minutes on a hot afternoon. If your event is outdoors, keep balloons in shade as long as possible, inflate them slightly under full size to allow for expansion, and use balloons with UV-protective treatment where available. And always warn the client: outdoor balloons are weather-dependent.
  • Children and small balloons: Uninflated or popped balloon pieces are a serious choking hazard for children under 8. Never leave small children unsupervised near balloon installations. Use large-format balloons at children's events and clean up any burst pieces immediately.
  • Helium balloon ceiling hazards: Helium balloons that escape indoors and press against light fittings or heating elements can be a fire risk. Always use balloon weights indoors and secure helium balloons properly.
  • Responsible disposal: Never release latex balloons into the atmosphere outdoors — they fragment into wildlife hazards. Dispose of deflated balloons in sealed rubbish bags. Foil balloons should never be released outdoors under any circumstances, as they conduct electricity and can cause power outages if they reach overhead lines.
  • Balloon popping sounds: Loud, sudden pops can distress children, elderly guests, and individuals with sensory sensitivities. Mention this in your event planning and try to pop and deflate leftover balloons away from the main gathering at the end of the night.

Frequently Asked Questions

Q1: How many balloons do I actually need for a balloon arch?

For a standard 6-foot organic balloon arch with good density, you will need approximately 80–120 balloons in mixed sizes. For a 10-foot arch, plan for 150–200. Most beginners underestimate this and end up with a sparse, underwhelming result. Buy 20% more than you think you need — leftover balloons are far less stressful than running short mid-build.

Q2: How long will balloon decorations last?

Air-filled latex balloon arrangements (arches, garlands, walls) typically look fresh for 1–2 weeks indoors in a stable temperature. Helium-filled latex balloons last 12–24 hours before floating low. Foil balloons filled with helium last 3–5 days. Bubble balloons with helium last 2–4 days. For events, I always recommend air-filling any structural pieces (arches, garlands) for longevity and reserving helium for bouquets and accent balloons.

Q3: Is it worth hiring a professional balloon decorator instead of DIYing?

It depends entirely on the scale and complexity of what you want. A small birthday garland for a home party is very achievable as a DIY project with some patience. A ceiling installation for a 100-person reception, a branded balloon wall for a corporate event, or anything requiring scaffolding or structural rigging is absolutely a job for a professional. The time, tools, and expertise involved are significant, and the risk of a collapsed or deflated installation mid-event is not worth the cost saving.

Q4: Can I make balloon decorations the day before the event?

Yes — with conditions. Air-filled latex balloon garlands and arches hold up well overnight if stored indoors away from heat, cold, and direct light. Do not make them more than 18–24 hours in advance. Helium-filled latex bouquets should be inflated on the morning of the event at the earliest — overnight floating is unreliable. Foil balloons can be inflated 1–2 days ahead without issues.

Q5: What is the best colour combination for a balloon arch in 2026?

The most popular professional combinations I am working with right now are: dusty rose, ivory, and champagne gold for weddings and bridal showers; sage green, terracotta, and cream for botanical-themed events; cobalt blue, white, and silver for corporate and formal celebrations; and bold primary palettes (red, yellow, blue) with white accents for children's parties. The key in 2026 is to include at least one neutral tone — clear, ivory, or cream — to stop colour combinations from feeling flat or over-saturated.

Q6: Are balloon decorations eco-friendly?

This is a nuanced answer. Natural latex is biodegradable, but the degradation process still takes longer than most people assume (up to four years), and balloon fragments are hazardous to wildlife before they fully break down. Foil and bubble balloons are not biodegradable at all. The most eco-conscious approach: use air-filled latex balloons (no helium waste, no floating hazard), dispose of all balloon pieces responsibly in sealed bags, choose reusable foil balloons over single-use latex where possible, and never release any balloon outdoors deliberately.

When to DIY vs Hire a Professional

After hundreds of balloon projects, here is my honest guide to when you should attempt it yourself and when you should call someone like us at Prettiee Decor:

Project Type DIY Friendly? Why
Small birthday garland (home party) ✅ Yes Low stakes, forgiving format, great starter project
Foil balloon bouquet or number display ✅ Yes No skill required, just inflate and tie
6-foot organic arch (first attempt) ⚠️ Maybe Achievable but plan for 3–4 hours and some frustration
Large balloon wall / backdrop (8+ feet) ❌ Hire a pro Requires volume, structural anchoring, and time you likely do not have
Ceiling balloon cloud or canopy ❌ Hire a pro Rigging and helium management are genuinely hazardous without experience
Corporate branded balloon installation ❌ Hire a pro Brand consistency, precision, and professional finish are non-negotiable
Wedding ceremony arch (100+ guests) ❌ Hire a pro Too much is riding on it; wedding morning stress makes DIY a risky choice

The rule I give every client: if you would be genuinely upset if it did not go perfectly, hire a professional. If you would laugh it off and enjoy the process, DIY it.

Final Thoughts: Balloons Are Worth It

After everything I have built and seen, I still get a little thrill when a balloon installation comes together exactly right — when the colours land perfectly, the arch holds its shape, and someone walks into the room and gasps. There is a joy in balloons that almost no other decoration medium can replicate. They are festive, immediate, and universal in a way that flowers and fabrics simply are not.

Whether you are blowing up a dozen balloons for a last-minute birthday at home or commissioning a full organic balloon ceiling for a wedding reception, the principles are the same: choose your palette carefully, invest in the right tools, know your limits, and do not underestimate how many balloons it takes to look truly abundant.

If you are looking for more celebration inspiration, here are a few resources from our blog that I think you will find genuinely useful:

  • Planning a birthday at home? Our comprehensive Birthday Party at Home Tips Guide covers everything from decoration layouts to budget-friendly entertainment ideas.
  • For couples planning a wedding and wanting a broader view of the full decoration picture, our Wedding Decoration Ideas 2026 Guide is a thorough companion to this post.

As always, if you want professional balloon decoration for your next celebration — whether it is an intimate bridal shower or a large-scale corporate launch — the team at Prettiee Decor is here to help you make it unforgettable.

— Harpreet Kaur, Lead Decorator at Prettiee Decor

title: Corporate Event Decoration Ideas in Dar es Salaam: A 2026 Guide for Brands That Want to Impress
author: Harpreet Kaur
excerpt: Product launches, galas, conferences and end-of-year parties - discover how to design corporate event decor in Dar es Salaam that stays on-brand, photographs beautifully, and impresses clients in 2026.
content:
Corporate Event Decoration Ideas in Dar es Salaam: A 2026 Guide for Brands That Want to Impress

There is a particular kind of pressure that comes with decorating a corporate event. A wedding is personal and forgiving. A corporate launch is judged by clients, investors, journalists, and every employee with a smartphone. The decor is not just decoration - it is a statement about how the brand sees itself. After designing dozens of corporate events across Dar es Salaam, from intimate boardroom product reveals to 400-guest end-of-year galas at beachfront resorts, I have learned that the brands that get this right treat their event space as an extension of their brand identity.

This guide breaks down exactly how to approach corporate event decoration in Dar es Salaam in 2026 - what works, what it costs, and how to make sure your event looks as professional as the business behind it.

Start With the Brand, Not the Decor

The single biggest mistake I see companies make is choosing decor they personally like rather than decor that reflects the brand. Before I select a single fabric or flower, I ask for three things: the brand's exact colour codes, the logo files, and an honest answer to one question - "What do you want guests to feel when they walk in?" Confident and premium? Approachable and energetic? Innovative and modern? That feeling drives every decision that follows.

A bank's annual gala should feel different from a tech startup's product launch. The bank wants gravitas - deep tones, structured arrangements, polished metallics. The startup wants energy - bold colour, dynamic lighting, playful installations. Both can be beautiful. Neither should borrow the other's playbook.

Decoration Ideas by Corporate Event Type

1. Product Launches

The product is the star - your decor exists to frame it, not compete with it. I keep the palette tight (usually the brand colours plus one neutral), build a strong focal backdrop for the reveal moment, and use directional lighting to draw every eye to the product.

  • Branded step-and-repeat backdrop for media photos and the reveal moment
  • Illuminated product plinths with uplighting to make the product glow
  • Custom neon or LED signage with the product name or campaign hashtag
  • Minimalist floral accents in brand colours - restraint reads as premium

2. Conferences and Seminars

Here, function leads. Guests sit for hours, so comfort and clear sightlines matter more than drama. The decor should reinforce the brand subtly without distracting from the speakers or screens.

  • Branded stage backdrop with the company logo and event theme
  • Lectern styling and a clean, professional speaker area
  • Registration and welcome desk styling - first impressions start at the door
  • Lounge breakout areas with comfortable seating for networking

3. Galas and Award Nights

This is where corporate decor gets to be glamorous. Galas justify full draping, dramatic centrepieces, layered lighting, and a real sense of occasion. The brand presence becomes more elegant and woven-in rather than loud.

  • Ceiling draping and chandeliers to transform large, plain halls
  • Tall statement centrepieces alternating with low arrangements across tables
  • Stage and award-presentation backdrop with elegant lighting
  • Custom table numbers and menus in brand fonts and colours

4. End-of-Year and Staff Parties

These should feel like a reward, not a meeting. Loosen up: warmer lighting, a festive palette, photo moments, and decor that encourages people to relax and celebrate. Staff parties are also where employees take the most photos - so give them backdrops worth posting.

  • Organic balloon installations in festive or brand colours
  • Themed photo booth with props and a styled backdrop
  • Warm Edison-bulb lighting and candle clusters for an intimate feel
  • Lounge vignettes for relaxed conversation away from the dance floor

Corporate Decor Budget Guide (Dar es Salaam, 2026)

Corporate budgets vary enormously, but here is a realistic breakdown of what companies spend on decoration for a mid-sized event of roughly 100-200 guests in Dar es Salaam. These figures cover decor and styling only - not catering, AV, or venue hire.

Decor Element Essential (TZS) Premium (TZS)
Branded stage / backdrop 800,000 - 1,800,000 3,000,000 - 7,000,000
Table centrepieces (per table) 35,000 - 70,000 120,000 - 300,000
Lighting (full venue) 600,000 - 1,500,000 2,500,000 - 6,000,000
Ceiling and wall draping 1,000,000 - 2,500,000 4,000,000 - 9,000,000
Registration / welcome area 300,000 - 700,000 1,200,000 - 3,000,000
Photo booth / brand activation 400,000 - 900,000 1,500,000 - 4,000,000

Note: Figures are 2026 estimates in Tanzanian Shillings and vary by venue, guest count, and complexity. Always request a detailed written quote before committing.

Five Rules for Corporate Decor That Actually Works

  1. Protect the brand colours. Match your exact Pantone or hex codes. "Close enough" blue is the fastest way to make an event look off-brand in photos.
  2. Design for the camera. Corporate events live on LinkedIn, Instagram, and in press coverage. Every key area - stage, entrance, photo wall - should be photo-ready from multiple angles.
  3. Invest in lighting first. A beautifully decorated hall under flat fluorescent light looks amateur. Warm, layered lighting elevates everything else you spend on.
  4. Keep walkways and sightlines clear. Guests need to network, reach the bar, and see the stage. Decoration should never block flow.
  5. Plan for setup and teardown windows. Many Dar venues have tight access times. Confirm load-in hours early so your decorator is not racing the clock.

Frequently Asked Questions

How far in advance should we book corporate event decor in Dar es Salaam?

For large galas and launches, book 2-3 months ahead - earlier for the November-December season, when corporate end-of-year events fill decorators' calendars quickly. Smaller boardroom events can often be arranged with 3-4 weeks' notice.

Can you match our exact brand colours?

Yes. Linens, drapes, balloons, and lighting can all be matched to brand colour codes. Provide your hex or Pantone values and logo files at the briefing stage and we build the palette around them.

Do you provide branded backdrops and signage?

Absolutely. Step-and-repeat media walls, stage backdrops, custom neon or LED signage, and branded welcome desks are among the most requested corporate elements - and the most valuable for photos and press.

Can decor be reused across multiple company events?

Many structural pieces - frames, draping, plinths, signage - can be reused or restyled for future events, which lowers the cost per event for companies that host regularly.

Let Prettiee Decor Handle Your Next Corporate Event

A corporate event is a brand opportunity disguised as a party. Done well, it strengthens client relationships, energises your team, and gives your marketing months of content. Done carelessly, it quietly undermines the professional image you have worked hard to build.

At Prettiee Decor, we design corporate events across Dar es Salaam that stay on-brand, run on schedule, and photograph beautifully - from product launches and conferences to galas and staff parties.

title: Baby Shower Decoration Ideas: A Complete 2026 Guide for a Celebration to Remember
author: Harpreet Kaur
excerpt: From gender-neutral themes to dreamy balloon backdrops and dessert tables, discover beautiful, budget-friendly baby shower decoration ideas you can DIY or have styled professionally in 2026.
content:
Baby Shower Decoration Ideas: A Complete 2026 Guide for a Celebration to Remember

A baby shower is one of the most joyful events I get to decorate. There is a softness to it - an anticipation and tenderness you do not find in any other celebration. The decor needs to match that feeling: gentle, warm, and welcoming, with just enough sparkle to make the mum-to-be feel celebrated. Over the years I have styled showers in living rooms, garden courtyards, and hotel function rooms, and the most memorable ones were never the most expensive. They were the most thoughtful.

This guide walks you through everything - themes, the must-have decor zones, a realistic budget, DIY tips, and the questions I get asked most. Whether you are planning it yourself or briefing a decorator, you will know exactly what to do.

Step One: Choose a Theme and Palette

Everything flows from the theme. Pick it first and the rest of the decisions become easy. Here are the directions trending in 2026:

  • Gender-neutral botanical: Sage green, ivory, and natural wood with eucalyptus and dried grasses. Calm, modern, and endlessly photogenic.
  • Cloud and sky: Soft blue, white, and silver with cloud-shaped foil balloons and gauzy draping. Dreamy and gender-neutral.
  • Classic blush: Dusty rose, cream, and gold - timeless for a baby girl without feeling overly pink.
  • Little prince / blue and gold: Navy, powder blue, and gold accents for a baby boy with a touch of elegance.
  • Boho neutral: Terracotta, caramel, and cream with pampas grass and macrame. Warm, earthy, and on-trend.

My advice: choose two main colours plus one neutral (ivory, cream, or clear). It keeps the look intentional rather than busy.

The Five Decor Zones Every Baby Shower Needs

I always plan a shower around five key zones. Get these right and the whole space feels complete - without decorating every square inch.

1. The Backdrop / Focal Wall

This is the heart of the shower and the most photographed spot. A balloon garland against a wall, a fabric draping panel, or a flower-and-greenery installation all work beautifully. Add the baby's name or "Oh Baby" in letter balloons or a wooden sign.

2. The Dessert and Cake Table

Style a table with the cake as the centrepiece, surrounded by cupcakes, cookies, and treats in your palette. A small balloon cluster, cake stands at varying heights, and a styled backdrop make this zone shine.

3. The Mum-to-Be's Chair

The guest of honour deserves a special seat. Decorate a feature chair with a balloon arch, a sash, florals, or a soft throw so she has a beautiful, comfortable place to open gifts and be photographed.

4. The Welcome and Guest Sign-In Area

Greet guests with a styled entrance - a small sign, a few balloons, and a guest book or "advice for the parents" station. It sets the tone the moment people arrive.

5. The Table Settings

Keep guest tables simple and coordinated: a runner in your accent colour, small floral or greenery touches, and themed napkins. Restraint here lets the backdrop and dessert table be the stars.

Baby Shower Budget Guide (2026)

One of the best things about baby showers is how flexible the budget can be. Here is a realistic breakdown for an intimate shower of around 20-40 guests.

Element DIY / Budget Professionally Styled
Backdrop / balloon garland $40 - $90 $150 - $400
Dessert table styling $30 - $70 $120 - $300
Mum-to-be feature chair $20 - $50 $80 - $200
Table settings (per table) $10 - $25 $35 - $90
Foil letters / name balloons $15 - $35 $40 - $90
Typical total $115 - $270 $425 - $1,080

DIY Tips That Make a Real Difference

  • Build the balloon garland the day before using air (not helium) so it holds its shape - store it flat indoors away from heat.
  • Use odd numbers when grouping props and treats. Threes and fives look more natural than pairs.
  • Create height variation on the dessert table with cake stands, stacked boxes under the cloth, or upturned bowls. Flat tables look dull.
  • Mix textures - pair smooth balloons with soft greenery, a knitted throw, or a woven basket for warmth.
  • Light it warmly. Even a few fairy lights or candles transform a daytime room into something special.

Frequently Asked Questions

When should a baby shower be held?

Most are held in the third trimester, usually 4-8 weeks before the due date - late enough to feel celebratory, early enough that the mum-to-be is comfortable and not too close to delivery.

How far ahead should I plan the decorations?

Start 3-4 weeks out: lock the theme and palette first, then order balloons, florals, and any custom signage. If hiring a decorator, book 2-3 weeks ahead, sooner for weekend dates.

What if we do not want to reveal the baby's gender?

Gender-neutral themes are more popular than ever. Botanical sage, cloud-and-sky, and boho neutral palettes are all beautiful and keep the surprise intact.

Can a baby shower be decorated at home?

Absolutely - homes make wonderfully intimate baby shower venues. Focus your effort on one strong backdrop and a styled dessert table, and the rest of the room follows naturally.

Let Us Style Your Baby Shower

A baby shower is a celebration of new life and the people who will love that little one. The decorations should make the mum-to-be feel cherished and give everyone beautiful memories to look back on.

title: How to Choose the Right Event Decorator in Tanzania: 9 Questions to Ask Before You Book
author: Harpreet Kaur
excerpt: Hiring the wrong decorator can derail your big day. Here are the 9 essential questions to ask, the red flags to watch for, and how to choose an event decorator in Tanzania you can truly trust in 2026.
content:
How to Choose the Right Event Decorator in Tanzania: 9 Questions to Ask Before You Book

I am going to give you advice that might seem strange coming from a decorator: choosing the right person to style your event matters more than the specific decorations themselves. I have been called in to rescue weddings and launches where another supplier let the client down - arrived late, delivered something that looked nothing like the agreed design, or simply vanished after taking a deposit. Those stories are why I wrote this guide. Whether you book us or someone else, I want you to choose wisely.

Here are the nine questions I would ask any decorator before handing over a deposit, plus the red flags that should make you pause.

The 9 Questions to Ask Every Decorator

1. Can I see a full portfolio of real, recent events?

Anyone can repost beautiful images from the internet. Ask for photos of events they personally delivered, ideally recent ones. A trustworthy decorator will happily show you a range of real work - and may even connect you with past clients.

2. Have you worked at my venue before?

A decorator familiar with your venue already knows its access times, power situation, ceiling heights, and quirks. If they have not worked there, ask whether they will do a site visit before the event. The good ones always do.

3. What exactly is included in the quote?

This is where most disputes begin. Get a written, itemised quote covering setup, teardown, transport, labour, and every decor element. "Full decoration for 2,000,000 TZS" is not a quote - it is a guess waiting to become an argument.

4. Do you have a backup plan for weather and power?

In Tanzania, outdoor events face real risks - rain in the wet seasons and occasional power interruptions. A professional has contingency plans: tents, generators, and lighting backups. If they look blank at this question, keep looking.

5. How many events do you take on per day?

A decorator juggling four weddings on the same Saturday cannot give yours full attention. Ask how many events they handle per day and who will physically be present at yours. You want to know your event is staffed, not squeezed in.

6. What is your payment and refund policy?

Understand the deposit amount, the payment schedule, and what happens if you need to cancel or postpone. Reasonable deposits are normal; demands for full payment upfront with no written terms are a warning sign.

7. Can you work within my budget honestly?

A good decorator will tell you honestly what your budget can and cannot achieve, then maximise it - rather than overpromising and cutting corners later. Beware anyone who agrees to everything without discussing trade-offs.

8. Will the setup match the design we agreed on?

Ask for a mood board, sketch, or mock-up so you both share the same vision. Confirm in writing that what you approved is what will be delivered. This single step prevents the most common and most heartbreaking disappointments.

9. What time will you arrive and finish setup?

Late setup is the number one complaint about unreliable decorators. Confirm the arrival time, the completion deadline (well before guests arrive), and teardown arrangements - in writing.

Red Flags to Watch For

Red Flag Why It Matters
No written contract or quote You have no protection if the delivery does not match the promise
Demands full payment upfront Reputable decorators work on deposits with staged payments
Cannot show real past work Borrowed portfolio images hide a lack of real experience
Vague or constantly changing pricing Often leads to surprise charges close to the event date
Poor or slow communication If they are hard to reach now, it gets worse under event pressure
No backup plan for weather/power A single rainstorm can ruin an unprepared outdoor event

Green Flags: Signs of a Decorator You Can Trust

  • They ask you lots of questions - about your vision, venue, guest count, and budget. Curiosity is a sign they care about getting it right.
  • They offer a site visit and a written, itemised proposal.
  • They are honest about trade-offs rather than promising everything for any budget.
  • They have verifiable reviews or referrals from real past clients.
  • They respond promptly and clearly from your very first message.

Frequently Asked Questions

How much should I budget for event decoration in Tanzania?

It varies widely by event type and scale. A modest home celebration may start around a few hundred thousand shillings, while a full wedding or corporate gala can run into the tens of millions. The key is matching your decorator to your budget honestly - and getting an itemised quote so you know exactly what you are paying for.

How early should I book a decorator?

For weddings and large events, 6-12 months ahead for peak-season dates. For smaller parties and corporate events, 3-6 weeks is often enough - though earlier is always safer for popular weekend dates.

Should I choose the cheapest decorator?

Be cautious. The cheapest quote often excludes items that reappear as surprise charges later, or reflects inexperience that shows up on the day. Look for the best value - quality, reliability, and clear pricing - not simply the lowest number.

What should be in a decoration contract?

An itemised list of decor elements, the agreed design or mood board, total cost and payment schedule, setup and teardown times, the cancellation/refund policy, and contact details for the person responsible on the day.

Work With a Decorator You Can Trust

Your event is too important to leave to chance. The right decorator does not just make things beautiful - they bring calm, reliability, and the quiet confidence that everything will be ready and perfect before your first guest arrives.

At Prettiee Decor, we pride ourselves on exactly that: real portfolios, honest quotes, written agreements, dependable setup times, and award-winning design. We would be delighted to answer every one of the questions above - and any others you have.
